summary: McKenzie discovers that a startup called HearABlog has been converting his blog posts into audio podcasts without his prior knowledge. He evaluates the quality positively, noting their skill at describing visual elements like screenshots and graphs. Rather than objecting to the unauthorized derivative work, he grants explicit blanket permission for them to continue. He later discovers they had actually tried to contact him but the email got lost during a busy period.
key_points: A startup created audio versions of his blog posts as a demo, which he discovered through referral logs
He appreciates the quality of the audio adaptations, particularly the oral descriptions of visual content
He grants blanket permission for the derivative works despite not being asked first, choosing to support cool projects
He corrects the pronunciation of his made-up company name Kalzumeus, noting even invented words have canonical pronunciations
The startup had actually emailed him for permission but the message was lost during his busy Halloween sales season
money_quotes: The quality is quite good — I'm especially impressed at their oral descriptions of the screenshots and graphs that were crucial to understanding some of those posts.
Because even made up words have a canonical pronunciation, even if only in my mind.

I'm not sure if strictly speaking creating derivative works from this blog is copyright kosher without asking me first, but in the interests of supporting people doing cool things, HearABlog has my explicit permission to produce and distribute audio recordings of all my blog posts on this site.
I have an email address and a marked aversion to saying 'No', so please, feel free to tell me about it in advance.
Unfortunately, he managed to email me when I was on a plane over the Pacific, and I didn't see the email later in the crush of payment notifications. A high class problem, to be sure.
key_insight: When someone creates something cool with your work, default to granting permission and supporting them rather than enforcing copyright — but gently suggest they ask first next time.
